## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, so this section of the secrets of leverage and scale is all about the big picture. This is the advanced stuff. This is how to get the life that you want. So the first thing I want to talk about is what I consider probably the most important thing in business, and that is opportunity cost. And so opportunity cost is the cost that you will have when you take on a project. What will taking on that project prevent you from doing? And often in pure business world, the opportunity cost is thought of as a financial thing. Like if you spend this million dollars to create this project, what product projects do you have to forego that you could have created with that million dollars? So I go and I spend this million dollars to create this great restaurant. What else could I have done with that million dollars? But in our kinds of businesses, which are generally not as capital intensive, but more time intensive, the same thing applies. In fact, more so because time is more limited than financial resources. And so if you take this project on, what project will you not be able to take on? What project are you giving up by taking this project on? If you take this client on, what are the clients are you going to have to say no to? There's this quote I love by Warren Buffett, who is probably the most successful investor ever. He's a billionaire many, many times over. And he says that very successful people, the difference between successful people and very successful business people, let me try this again. The difference between successful people and really successful people is that really successful people say no to almost everything. Now saying no doesn't come easy to very many of us. To most of us, we have a hard time saying no, but that's the thing you always have to say, what is the opportunity cost here? My friend, Dean Greziosi, he puts it, he thinks about he's sitting in his office and behind him is a bookcase of his favorite books. He just loves these books. He cherishes these books and the bookcase is completely full. Someone comes in and they have just a wonderful book or a book they're trying to sell you or they're trying to give you or they're trying to get you to consume. And he looks at that and says, if I take

One of the things that you might consider is focusing on what's the value of a focus day. So focus day is something I learned from Dan Sullivan. And that's where he does sort of the get stuck doing the nitty gritty, so

## [06:01 - 09:06]

to speak, of the business. The pain in the neck stuff that all of us seem stuck doing, you know, the filing and the taxes and administrative stuff. So he has free days and buffer days and hopefully there aren't very many buffer days, but then there's also focus days. And that's where you're doing your genius work. This is all Dan Sullivan 101. And one of the things that's valuable is to calculate what's the value of a focus day because the reality is you don't have that many focus days in a year.
